explanation: This grammar is used when someone quoted or said something, but the actual result turned out different or contrasting. It combines indirect quotation of a present-tense statement with 더니, which shows a turn of events. Use it when you want to say "They said X, but (in reality) Y."

rules.title: Action verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → V-ㄴ다더니
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다더니 to the stem. This form is used when quoting an action that was said to happen.

examples.english: 친구가 온다더니 안 왔어요 — They said a friend was coming, but they didn't come.
케이크를 만든다더니 사 온 거예요 — They said they'd make a cake, but they bought one instead.

rules.title: Action verbs with consonant ending → V-는다더니
rules.description: Attach 는다더니 to the stem. Use this for action verbs that end in a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 다 먹는다더니 남겼더라고요 — They said they'd eat it all, but they left some.
책을 읽는다더니 자꾸 전화만 해요 — They said they'd read, but they keep making phone calls.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → A-다더니
rules.description: Attach 다더니 directly to the adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction for adjectives.

examples.english: 그 집이 작다더니 생각보다 컸어요 — They said that house was small, but it was bigger than I thought.
맛있다더니 먹어 보니 별로였어요 — They said it was delicious, but when I tried it, it was nothing special.

rules.title: Noun + 이다 → 이라더니 / 아니라더니
rules.description: For nouns with 이다, use 이라더니. For 아니다, use 아니라더니.

examples.english: 학생이라더니 벌써 졸업했대요 — They said (he) was a student, but I heard he already graduated.

notes.title: Contrast is key
notes.text: The second clause usually describes a result that contrasts with the quoted statement. However, it can also confirm what was said, though this is less common.

notes.title: Speaker's direct observation
notes.text: The result in the second clause must be something the speaker directly observed or learned about firsthand.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also 302 (A/V-(으)ㄴ/는다니까) for giving reasons based on hearsay, and 303 for observed results.

explanation: Use this when giving a reason or cause based on what someone else said. It combines indirect quotation of a present statement with 니까 ("because"). It translates to "They say... so..." or "Because they say..." Use it to pass along someone's statement as justification for an action or conclusion.

rules.title: Action verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → V-ㄴ다니까
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다니까 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 친구가 온다니까 기다리세요 — They say a friend is coming, so please wait.
곧 시작한다니까 서둘러요 — They say it'll start soon, so hurry.

rules.title: Action verbs with consonant ending → V-는다니까
rules.description: Attach 는다니까 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 끝난다니까 이제 쉬어요 — They say it's ending, so rest now.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → A-다니까
rules.description: Attach 다니까 directly to the adjective stem.

examples.english: 시험이 어렵다니까 열심히 공부하세요 — They say the exam is hard, so study hard.
날씨가 춥다니까 따뜻하게 입으세요 — They say the weather is cold, so dress warmly.

notes.title: Relaying instructions
notes.text: This is commonly used to pass along instructions, warnings, or advice from a third party.

notes.title: Quoted information as reason
notes.text: The quoted statement is treated as the direct cause for the command, suggestion, or statement in the second clause.

explanation: Use these endings when you heard someone say something and you either observed it to be true (다더라) or recall hearing it (다던데). 다더라 emphasizes that you observed the result yourself, while 다던데 is softer and more retrospective — it sets up background information or seeks mild agreement.

rules.title: Action verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → V-ㄴ다더라 / V-ㄴ다던데
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다더라 or ㄴ다던데 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 그 사람이 내일 온다더라 — They said that person is coming tomorrow (and I observed the situation).
비가 온다던데 우산을 챙기세요 — They said it's going to rain, so take an umbrella.

rules.title: Action verbs with consonant ending → V-는다더라 / V-는다던데
rules.description: Attach 는다더라 or 는다던데 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 수업이 곧 시작한다더라 — They said class is starting soon (and I confirmed it).

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → A-다더라 / A-다던데
rules.description: Attach 다더라 or 다던데 directly to the adjective stem.

examples.english: 그 집이 맛있다더라 — They said that place is delicious (and I found it to be true).
요즘 바쁘다던데 연락해 보세요 — They said they're busy these days, so try contacting them.

notes.title: Direct realization vs. soft recall
notes.text: 다더라 carries a sense of personal realization — you heard it and then saw it with your own eyes. 다던데 is softer, used to pass on what you heard as background.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also 301 (A/V-(으)ㄴ/는다더니) for contrasting results, and 315 (V-던데) for retrospective observation.

explanation: Use this when you want to express surprise, disbelief, or a strong reaction to something you heard. It combines quoted speech with 다니, which adds an emotional punch. It translates to "They say... (can you believe it?)" or "(I heard that)... (surprising!)."

rules.title: Action verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → V-ㄴ다니
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다니 to the verb stem. Use this when reacting with surprise to news about an action.

examples.english: 그 사람이 한국에 간다니 믿을 수 없어요 — I can't believe they say that person is going to Korea.
회사를 그만둔다니 정말이에요? — They say (he's) quitting the company — is it true?

rules.title: Action verbs with consonant ending → V-는다니
rules.description: Attach 는다니 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 시험에 떨어진다니 너무 슬프다 — They say (he) failed the exam; how sad.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → A-다니
rules.description: Attach 다니 directly to the adjective stem.

examples.english: 그녀가 예쁘다니 처음 들었어요 — I heard for the first time that they say she's pretty.
그 문제가 쉽다니 정말요? — They say that problem is easy — really?

notes.title: Emotionally charged
notes.text: 다니 is emotionally loaded — use it for surprise, joy, sadness, or disbelief. It is not neutral.

notes.title: Common collocations
notes.text: Often used with 믿을 수 없다, 놀랍다, 슬프다, or 처음 들었다 to reinforce the emotional reaction.

explanation: This means "even if they say..." or "no matter how much they claim...". It dismisses or downplays what someone says, emphasizing that the result or the speaker's stance won't change. Use it when you want to express skepticism or firmness despite someone's words.

rules.title: Action verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → V-ㄴ다고 해도
rules.description: Attach ㄴ다고 해도 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 늦는다고 해도 기다릴 거예요 — Even if they say they'll be late, I'll wait.

rules.title: Action verbs with consonant ending → V-는다고 해도
rules.description: Attach 는다고 해도 to the verb stem.

examples.english: 그렇게 말한다고 해도 안 믿어요 — Even if they say that, I don't believe it.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs → A-다고 해도
rules.description: Attach 다고 해도 directly to the adjective stem.

examples.english: 시험이 어렵다고 해도 열심히 봐야 해요 — Even if they say the exam is hard, I have to try my best.
괜찮다고 해도 병원에 가 보세요 — Even if they say it's fine, go to the hospital.

notes.title: Unchanged stance
notes.text: This grammar emphasizes that the speaker's decision or the situation remains unchanged regardless of what is claimed.

notes.title: Firm tone
notes.text: It can sound firm or even stubborn depending on intonation and context. Use carefully in sensitive conversations.

explanation: This is a formal, written-style connective meaning "therefore," "because," or "as." Use it in official documents, essays, presentations, and formal speeches to show a logical cause-and-effect relationship between two clauses.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 므로
rules.description: Attach 므로 directly to the stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 시간이 없으므로 서둘러야 합니다 — There is no time, therefore we must hurry.
이 길이 길므로 차를 타고 가세요 — As this road is long, please go by car.

rules.title: Consonant ending (except ㄹ) → + 으므로
rules.description: Attach 으므로 to stems ending in a consonant other than ㄹ.

examples.english: 그 말이 맞으므로 따르겠습니다 — As those words are correct, I will comply.
늦으므로 미리 출발하겠습니다 — As we are late, we will depart early.

notes.title: Formal register only
notes.text: This is much more formal than -아/어서 or -(으)니까. Avoid it in casual conversation.

notes.title: Second clause type
notes.text: The second clause is usually a statement, plan, or formal request — not a raw command ending in -아/어.

explanation: Use this sentence ending when giving an explanation, reason, or background for what you just said or did. It translates to "you see" or "the thing is." It softens statements and invites the listener to understand your perspective. The 요 form (거든요) is common in polite conversation.

rules.title: V/A-거든(요)
rules.description: Attach 거든(요) directly to the verb or adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 내일 가거든요 — You see, I'm going tomorrow.
피곤하거든요 — You see, I'm tired.
바쁘거든요 — You see, I'm busy.

notes.title: Proactive explanation
notes.text: 거든(요) cannot be used to answer a direct question. It proactively provides a reason before being asked.

notes.title: Conversational softness
notes.text: It is softer and more conversational than -아/어서 or -(으)니까 when ending a sentence.

explanation: This mid-sentence connective has two main uses. First, it sets up a condition: "if [something happens], then..." Second, it gives a reason or cause for what follows. In both cases, the second clause is typically a command, suggestion, or question. It is more common in written or slightly formal contexts than casual speech.

rules.title: V-거든 (conditional "if")
rules.description: Use 거든 in the first clause when the second clause is a command, suggestion, or question. It means "if."

examples.english: 한국에 가거든 부산도 들러 봐 — If you go to Korea, visit Busan too.
그 영화를 보거든 후기 좀 알려 줘 — If you watch that movie, let me know what you think.

rules.title: V-거든 (reason "because")
rules.description: Use 거든 to give a reason that leads to a command or suggestion in the second clause.

examples.english: 늦거든 미리 연락해 — Because we're late, contact them in advance.
바쁘거든 나중에 해 — Because it's busy, do it later.

notes.title: Second clause restriction
notes.text: When used as "if," the second clause is almost always an imperative, propositive, or question. A plain statement in the second clause is unnatural.

notes.title: Not a sentence ending
notes.text: 거든 without 요 is strictly a mid-sentence connective. Do not end a sentence with 거든 alone in standard Korean.

explanation: Use this casual connective when you did something because you observed or noticed a situation. It often implies a spontaneous reaction based on what you saw or felt. It can carry a slight sense of complaint or simple explanation. The subject of the main clause is usually the speaker.

rules.title: V/A-길래
rules.description: Attach 길래 directly to the verb or adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 친구가 가길래 따라갔어요 — My friend was going, so I followed.
비가 오길래 우산을 가져갔어요 — It was raining, so I took an umbrella.
말이 없길래 무슨 일이 있는 줄 알았어요 — He was so quiet, I thought something was wrong.

notes.title: Very casual
notes.text: 길래 is extremely casual. Do not use it in formal writing or with superiors.

notes.title: Speaker's action
notes.text: The main clause usually describes the speaker's own action or judgment. Using it for someone else's action sounds odd.

explanation: This formal connective means "since" or "as," and it emphasizes that the result matches the degree of the cause. It combines reason (니) with extent (만큼). Use it when you want to say that because A is true, B should follow to the same degree.

rules.title: Vowel or ㄹ ending → + 니만큼
rules.description: Attach 니만큼 directly to the stem when it ends in a vowel or ㄹ.

examples.english: 열심히 공부하니만큼 좋은 성적을 받을 거예요 — Since you study hard, you will get good grades.
오래 기다리니만큼 보람이 있을 거예요 — Since you've waited so long, it will be rewarding.

rules.title: Consonant ending → + 으니만큼
rules.description: Attach 으니만큼 to stems ending in a consonant.

examples.english: 잘 먹으니만큼 건강해요 — Since you eat well, you're healthy.
당신 말이 맞으니만큼 따르겠습니다 — As your words are correct, I will follow.

notes.title: Proportionality
notes.text: 니만큼 is more formal than 니까 and emphasizes that the result should match the effort or degree of the cause.

notes.title: Common contexts
notes.text: It is common in speeches, essays, and formal requests where cause and effect should be proportional.

exceptions.name: ㅂ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㅂ, change ㅂ to 우 before ㄴ.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄹ, drop ㄹ before ㄴ.

explanation: This literary and formal connective means "even if." It is used to concede a point while emphasizing that the result remains unchanged. It carries a slightly resigned or rhetorical tone, often used in proverbs, formal writing, or dramatic speech. It is more formal and less common in everyday conversation than -아/어도.

rules.title: Action verbs → V-는들
rules.description: Attach 는들 to the verb stem. There is no 받침 distinction for action verbs.

examples.english: 지금 가는들 늦지 않을까요? — Even if we go now, won't we be late?
열심히 하는들 결과가 똑같아요 — Even if I work hard, the result is the same.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs with consonant ending → A-(으)ㄴ들
rules.description: Attach (으)ㄴ들 to the adjective stem. Use -은들 after a consonant (except ㄹ).

examples.english: 돈이 많은들 다 행복합니까? — Even if one has a lot of money, is one happy?
날씨가 좋은들 갈 수가 없어요 — Even if the weather is good, I can't go.

rules.title: Descriptive verbs with vowel or ㄹ ending → A-ㄴ들
rules.description: Attach -ㄴ들 after a vowel or ㄹ-ending adjective stem.

examples.english: 집이 큰들 혼자서 다 쓸 수 있어요? — Even if the house is big, can you use it all alone?
길이 긴들 걸어갈 수 있어요 — Even if the road is long, we can walk.

notes.title: Rhetorical tone
notes.text: -(으)ㄴ/는들 is often used in rhetorical questions or philosophical statements. It sounds more dramatic than -아/어도.

notes.title: Unchanged outcome
notes.text: The second clause frequently contains a negative or unchanged outcome to contrast with the concession.

exceptions.name: ㄹ irregular
exceptions.description: For stems ending in ㄹ, drop ㄹ before ㄴ.

explanation: Use this connective when you observed something in the past and want to describe what happened next — usually a change, contrast, or result. It connects a past observation (retrospective) to a present situation. The subject of both clauses is typically the same when showing personal change, or different when showing cause and effect.

rules.title: V-더니 (same subject — change over time)
rules.description: Attach 더니 to the verb or adjective stem. Use this when the same person or thing shows a change from past to present.

examples.english: 어제는 춥더니 오늘은 따뜻하네요 — Yesterday it was cold, but today it's warm.
그때는 그렇게 말하더니 이제 와서 후회해요 — Back then you said that, but now you regret it.

rules.title: V-더니 (different subjects — cause and result)
rules.description: Use this when someone else's observed action leads to a result or new situation for the speaker or another subject.

examples.english: 친구가 전화하더니 기분이 좋아졌어요 — My friend called, and I felt better.
버스가 늦더니 지각했어요 — The bus was late, so I was tardy.

notes.title: Factual second clause
notes.text: 더니 cannot be used when the second clause is a command, suggestion, or intention. It must be a factual statement.

notes.title: Retrospective observation
notes.text: The first clause must be something the speaker personally observed in the past. You cannot use it for things you only heard about.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also 315 (V-던데) for sharing background observations, and 316 (V-더라) for direct observation endings.

explanation: This means "even if" and is used for hypothetical or imagined situations. It is more hypothetical and less certain than -아/어도, which often describes actual attempts. Use it to talk about things that haven't happened yet or are unlikely, emphasizing that the result would still hold.

rules.title: V/A-더라도
rules.description: Attach 더라도 directly to the verb or adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 비가 오더라도 가야 해요 — Even if it rains, I have to go.
힘이 들더라도 포기하지 마세요 — Even if it's hard, don't give up.
실패하더라도 다시 시도하세요 — Even if you fail, try again.

notes.title: Hypothetical nuance
notes.text: 더라도 is softer and more hypothetical than -아/어도. It imagines a scenario rather than testing it through actual experience.

notes.title: Determination and advice
notes.text: It often appears with expressions of determination, reassurance, or advice to show unwavering stance.

explanation: Use this when recalling something you observed in the past and using it as background for what you are saying now. It can appear mid-sentence (던데) to set up context, or at the end (던데요) to share an observation and invite agreement. It carries a warm, reflective tone.

rules.title: V-던데(요)
rules.description: Attach 던데(요) directly to the verb or adjective stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 그가 김치를 먹던데 맛있나 봐요 — He was eating kimchi; it must be delicious.
아기가 자던데 깨우지 마세요 — The baby was sleeping, so don't wake him.
그녀가 노래하던데 목소리가 참 좋아요 — She was singing, and her voice is really good.

notes.title: Retrospective observation
notes.text: 던데(요) is retrospective: you must have seen or experienced the action yourself. It cannot be used for secondhand information.

notes.title: Mid-sentence vs. ending
notes.text: Mid-sentence 던데 sets up background for your next point. Sentence-ending 던데요 softly shares an observation and invites agreement.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also 316 (V-더라) for direct observation endings, and 317 (V-던가) for uncertain recall.

explanation: Use these sentence endings when reporting something you directly observed or experienced. 더라 is a plain, matter-of-fact observation. 더군요 adds a sense of realization, mild surprise, or new understanding. Both are only used when the speaker personally witnessed the situation.

rules.title: V-더라
rules.description: Attach 더라 directly to the verb or adjective stem. Use this for neutral, factual observations.

examples.english: 그 사람이 학교에 가더라 — I noticed that person going to school.
사람이 참 많더라 — There were so many people (I saw).

rules.title: V-더군(요)
rules.description: Attach 더군(요) to the stem. Use this when the observation brings a sense of realization or mild surprise.

examples.english: 그 집이 참 예쁘더군요 — That house was really pretty, I noticed.
그가 많이 먹더군요 — He ate a lot (I observed).

notes.title: First-person only
notes.text: These endings are strictly first-person. You cannot use them to report what the listener observed.

notes.title: Storytelling and diaries
notes.text: They are common in storytelling, diary entries, and sharing personal experiences with friends.

notes.title: Related grammar
notes.text: See also 315 (V-던데) for sharing background observations with context.

explanation: Use these when you are trying to recall a past observation but feel uncertain or vague about the details. 던가(요)? is question-like: "I wonder if..." or "Was it...?" 던데(요) is statement-like but soft and uncertain: "I seem to recall that..." Both express the speaker's fuzzy memory about something they think they saw or heard.

rules.title: V-던가(요)?
rules.description: Attach 던가(요)? to the stem. Use this when wondering aloud about a past observation.

examples.english: 그 사람이 거기 가던가요? — I wonder if that person was going there?
아기가 벌써 자던가? — I wonder if the baby was already asleep?

rules.title: V-던데(요)
rules.description: Attach 던데(요) to the stem. Use this when making a soft, uncertain statement of recollection.

examples.english: 그가 김치를 먹던데 — I seem to recall he was eating kimchi.
거기에 있던데 — I think it was there.

notes.title: Soft and tentative
notes.text: These forms are softer and more tentative than direct questions or statements. They are useful when you want to mention something without claiming certainty.

notes.title: Seeking confirmation
notes.text: Use 던가(요)? when you want the listener to confirm or correct your vague memory.

explanation: Use this when an action was repeated or continued over time, and as a natural result, something new was realized or happened. It translates to "after doing something repeatedly..." or "as I kept doing...". The (가) is optional — adding it slightly emphasizes the ongoing process.

rules.title: V-다(가) 보니(까)
rules.description: Attach 다(가) 보니(까) directly to the verb stem. There is no 받침 distinction.

examples.english: 한국에 자주 가다 보니 한국어가 늘었어요 — I went to Korea often, and my Korean improved.
그 노래를 많이 듣다 보니 좋아지더라고요 — I listened to that song a lot, and I came to like it.
서울에서 살다가 보니 교통이 익숙해졌어요 — Living in Seoul, I got used to the traffic.

notes.title: Same subject required
notes.text: The subject of both clauses must be the same — it describes the speaker's own gradual realization or change.

notes.title: Unintentional result
notes.text: The result is usually something unintentional or naturally discovered, not a planned outcome.

notes.title: Optional 가
notes.text: -다 보니 and -다가 보니 are both correct. Adding 가 puts slightly more emphasis on the ongoing process.
